1.synthesis: The sin offering is applied to particular cases — failing to testify, touching the unclean, or rash oaths (vv.1–4). When guilty, the person must confess “the thing in which he has sinned” and bring an offering (v.5); and crucially, God scales the offering to ability: a lamb or goat, or for the poor two birds, or for the very poor “a tenth of an ephah of fine flour” (vv.7, 11). The integrative thread is confession met by accommodating grace: real guilt must be named and atoned for, yet God so provides that even the poorest may be cleansed, ensuring no one is shut out of forgiveness for want of means.
1.historical_context: The specified sins (vv.1–4) cover failures of testimony, contracted ceremonial uncleanness, and thoughtless vows — ordinary lapses of daily life. The requirement to “confess” (v.5) makes verbal acknowledgment of the specific sin part of the rite. The graded provision down to fine flour (v.11) — a bloodless sin offering for the destitute — is a striking mercy, though even this is associated with the altar where blood is regularly shed.
1.literary_structure: This section moves from the general sin-offering theology of chapter 4 to concrete cases, adding the element of confession (v.5) and the descending scale of offerings (vv.7–11). The accommodation to poverty echoes the same pattern in the burnt offering (Leviticus 1), reinforcing the book’s consistent provision for all means. It bridges toward the guilt offering of v.14.
1.theological_application: Forgiveness is tied to honest confession of specific sin (v.5; 1 John 1:9, “if we confess our sins, he is faithful… to forgive”), not vague regret. The scaling of the offering to ability (v.7, v.11) reveals a God who removes every barrier to forgiveness for the poor, so that grace is genuinely accessible to all. The believer learns to name sin plainly before God and to trust that his provision of cleansing excludes no one willing to come.
1.christology: The bloodless flour offering for the poorest still depended on the altar of blood (Hebrews 9:22), pointing beyond every graded sacrifice to the one offering that truly takes away sin (Hebrews 10:4, “it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins”). Christ is the sin offering for rich and poor alike, confessed and received by faith (Romans 10:9–10). The confession the law required is answered by the One who bears the sins we name, cleansing all who come to him empty-handed. The graded provision culminating in a bloodless flour offering for the destitute, yet still bound to the altar of blood (Hebrews 9:22), shows the system straining toward a sufficiency it could not itself supply. Only Christ, the sin offering for rich and poor alike, truly takes away sin (Hebrews 10:11–14). The confession the law required (Leviticus 5:5) is answered by the One who receives all who name their sin and flee to him for cleansing (1 John 1:9).

14.synthesis: The guilt offering addresses sins involving “a breach of faith” and damage requiring restitution — wrongs against “the holy things of the LORD” (v.15). The offender brings a ram “without blemish,” makes “restitution for what he has done amiss… and shall add a fifth to it,” and the priest makes atonement, “and he shall be forgiven” (v.16). The integrative thread is atonement joined to reparation: where sin has caused measurable harm or defrauded what is sacred, forgiveness comes not only through sacrifice but through making the wrong right with added compensation — true repentance restores, and grace does not bypass justice but satisfies it.
14.historical_context: The guilt or “trespass” offering (Hebrew asham, v.15) dealt with sins that incurred a debt — misappropriating holy things or defrauding a neighbor (Leviticus 6:2–5) — and uniquely required restitution plus a twenty-percent penalty (v.16). The ram was the prescribed animal. This offering linked the vertical (atonement before God) with the horizontal (repayment to the wronged party), uniting worship and justice.
14.literary_structure: The guilt offering (v.14–6:7) is distinguished from the sin offering by its emphasis on reparation and the added fifth (v.16). The provision for the “if he did not know” case (vv.17–19) extends atonement even to suspected but unconfirmed guilt, showing the thoroughness of the system in addressing every shade of culpability.
14.theological_application: Genuine repentance makes restitution: where we have wronged God or neighbor, forgiveness is sought alongside the concrete repair of the damage, with generous overpayment (v.16; Luke 19:8, Zacchaeus). Grace does not nullify justice or excuse us from righting our wrongs (Matthew 5:23–24). The believer learns that confession without restitution, where restitution is possible, is incomplete, and that God’s forgiveness frees us to make amends rather than to evade them. The guilt offering’s union of atonement with restitution (v.16) teaches that repentance toward God and the repair of wrongs toward others belong together; we cannot claim forgiveness while refusing to right the harm we have caused where we are able (Matthew 5:23–24). The added fifth shows that restored relationship may cost more than the original loss, and that grace, far from excusing us from making amends, frees and moves us to do so generously (Luke 19:8).
14.christology: Isaiah’s great Servant prophecy names the suffering Messiah’s life as an asham, a guilt offering: “when his soul makes an offering for guilt… he shall bear their iniquities” (Isaiah 53:10–11). Christ is the guilt offering who makes full restitution for the debt of sin we could never repay, “restoring what he did not steal” (Psalm 69:4) and cancelling the record of debt against us (Colossians 2:14). In him both atonement and the satisfaction of justice are perfectly accomplished.
